food_subgroup: Grilled glutinous rice parcel wrapped in banana leaf with spiced prawn and coconut sambal filling; traditional Malay kuih

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Pulut Panggang or Malay glutinous rice kuih. No defensible exact URI available; closest parent would be a generic glutinous rice dish node.
ontology.foodex2_description: Glutinous rice-based dish (closest parent: rice-based composite dish). FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Pulut Panggang; A000E (composite rice dish) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Pulut Panggang. A000E used as closest defensible parent for a composite rice-based dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Pulut Panggang or equivalent Malay kuih preparation.

preparation_method: steamed (glutinous rice pre-cooked with coconut milk)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Pulut Panggang weighs approximately 100g and constitutes a typical single serving as sold at Malay kuih stalls and pasar malam night markets.

nutrient_source.title: Pulut Panggang — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data and IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Pulut Panggang. Values represent a composite of glutinous rice cooked with coconut milk and prawn sambal filling. Per 100g values are consistent with published HPB and IMR data for this traditional kuih preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Glutinous Rice — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~98 for white glutinous rice (waxy rice, virtually pure amylopectin) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Pulut Panggang uses white glutinous rice as its primary carbohydrate base; GI is applied directly from the glutinous rice entry. GL per 100g serving = (98 × 33.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 32.83, rounded to 32.8. This is consistent with published GI data for glutinous rice preparations including Lo Mai Gai and similar dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350mg per 100g piece, primarily from dried shrimp (udang kering) in the sambal filling
hypertension.guidance: One piece of Pulut Panggang contributes 350mg sodium (~15% of the 2,300mg daily limit); hypertensive patients should limit to one piece and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~98 (very high) and GL ~32.8 per piece due to white glutinous rice being virtually pure amylopectin
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pulut Panggang is strongly discouraged for T2DM patients; a single 100g piece delivers a glycemic load of ~32.8 — equivalent to a full bowl of white rice — and two pieces would exceed GL 65, making blood glucose management extremely difficult.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.5g per 100g piece from coconut milk, combined with 30mg cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (5.5g per piece, ~28% of a 20g daily limit) from coconut milk warrants moderation for cardiovascular disease patients; occasional consumption of one piece is acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern, but regular intake should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pulut Panggang is a traditional Malay kuih (snack) consisting of glutinous rice (pulut) cooked with coconut milk, filled with a spiced prawn and grated coconut sambal, wrapped tightly in a banana leaf, and grilled over charcoal or on a pan grill. The grilling imparts a distinctive smoky, slightly charred aroma from the banana leaf to the outer rice layer, while the interior remains soft and fragrant. The prawn sambal filling typically contains dried shrimp (udang kering), fresh prawns, chilli, shallots, and grated coconut, providing a savoury-spicy contrast to the rich, creamy glutinous rice. One standard piece weighs approximately 100g and provides 215 kcal, 5g protein, 7.5g fat (5.5g saturated from coconut milk), and 33.5g carbohydrates. Nutritionally, the most critical feature is the extremely high glycemic index (~98) of white glutinous rice, which is composed almost entirely of amylopectin — a rapidly digestible starch. A single piece delivers a glycemic load of ~32.8, equivalent in glycemic impact to a full bowl of steamed white rice. This makes Pulut Panggang one of the highest-GL snack foods available at Malay kuih stalls and pasar malam (night markets). For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, Pulut Panggang is strongly discouraged; two pieces would produce a combined GL exceeding 65. The sodium content (350mg per piece) from dried shrimp requires moderation for hypertensive patients. The saturated fat from coconut milk (5.5g per piece) is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Pulut Panggang is widely available at Malay kuih stalls, pasar malam night markets, and during Hari Raya celebrations. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore and IMR Malaysia MyFCD, which are the primary approved regional sources for Singaporean and Malaysian dishes. GI value of ~98 for white glutinous rice is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent across multiple peer-reviewed publications. The food is a well-documented traditional Malay kuih with stable composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Pulut Panggang is not separately reported in HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases; only total fat and saturated fat are available. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this grilled glutinous rice preparation is not reported in the HPB or IMR source data. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; confirmed per HPB source data). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients; consistent with HPB data for plant-based and shellfish-based preparations of this type). FoodEx2 code A000E used as closest defensible parent for composite rice-based dish; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for Pulut Panggang. GI of 98 applied from University of Sydney GI Database entry for white glutinous rice (waxy rice); this is an analogous estimate as no direct GI test of Pulut Panggang as a composite dish has been published, but the glutinous rice base dominates the glycemic response.
